title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure-related items including a transportation funding resolution, a recycled asphalt crushing contract, and construction service proposals. Staff reported on receiving a $800,000+ grant for Birch Street reconstruction and discussed ongoing road repairs, utility projects, and a significant sanitary sewer clear water infiltration problem costing the village tens of thousands in extra treatment costs.

agenda.item: Call to order
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comment
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
CIP update
Street operations update
Utility operations update
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Resolution 2026-014 transportation funding
Recycled base course production contract
Sanitary sewer discharge violation discussion
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue duplex
Military Road construction agreement

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. The project will be moved from 2029 to 2028. He noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue road settlement repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating, and Schofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction including manhole and concrete work.

Staff reported on spring road activities including removing road limits on April 29th, mailbox repairs, sign repairs, and shoulder damage repairs. Significant discussion occurred about flooding issues near Weston Avenue requiring dewatering pumps for approximately one week. Committee members discussed potential permanent pump solutions and drainage improvements. Spring sweeping, patching with hot mix, and storm damage cleanup were completed. A culvert failure on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and ongoing sanitary sewer issues. A significant clear water infiltration problem was identified - in April, the village pumped 37 million gallons into the water system but treated nearly 54 million gallons at the wastewater plant, meaning approximately 15 million gallons of clear water entered the sanitary system. This is attributed to sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers and groundwater infiltration. An INI study with In-Pipe Technologies is planned to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure added. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term funding solutions for the state transportation fund, noting that while grant funding helps one-time costs, recurring annual costs for street maintenance and snow plowing are inadequately funded by the state. The committee voted to recommend the village board adopt the resolution. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23 per ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material, totaling $126,900. He noted this program saves the village approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to purchasing material, resulting in about $130,000 savings across the three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on fining residents who illegally discharge clear water (primarily sump pumps) into the sanitary sewer system. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee members discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. Michael noted March treatment costs were $30,000 higher than last year and April was $42,000 higher due to excess flow. Committee directed staff to develop enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100 per day. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on Lot 1 at 2230 Radkey Avenue, which currently lacks water/sewer main frontage. Staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral connection until Radkey Avenue is reconstructed. Committee amended the agreement to specify reconstruction no later than 2029, at which time the owner will pay assessment costs for main extension. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share covers utility relocation costs of $94,860 ($6,156 for sanitary sewer, $88,704 for water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services on two projects. For the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project, MSA submitted the low proposal at $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA was lower at $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730, but staff recommended Clark Dietz due to having a full-time experienced inspector rather than a summer intern. Committee approved MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue. Motion passed unanimously.

actionItems: Approve Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding solutions
Award recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Staff to develop sanitary sewer discharge violation enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day
Approve Radkey Avenue water/sewer connection agreement with reconstruction deadline no later than 2029
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860 in utility costs
Award Alderson/Jelinek roundabout construction services to MSA ($37,315)
Award Bladel Avenue construction services to Clark Dietz ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting scheduled for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday this week

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission addressing building orientation challenges due to a gas pipeline easement.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
April 2026 building permits
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Commissioner remarks
Future agenda items and staff remarks
Adjournment

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, noting it meets all B2 zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a 36-inch gas pipeline running through the property, which significantly reduced fill costs. The building is designed for 50-60 officer capacity versus the current 40 officers. Discussion included an alternate storage shed placement, with commissioners suggesting moving it north to accommodate future expansion. A motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT requirements. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg that predates the wellhead protection ordinance and has never gone through the conditional use process. Commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Motion to approve the program with reporting to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously without discussion.

Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites. Discussion touched on potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with commissioners requesting Ehlers' presentation on residential TIF qualifications be shared, possibly incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He requested the commission urge staff to waive excessive landscaping requirements for the police department project similar to what was done for the Greenheck Turner building to save taxpayer dollars. He also asked that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on inspections conducted and results, noting some zoning complaints are years old and get 'lost in the sauce.'
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation with reporting to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that predates wellhead protection ordinance about compliance
Staff to conduct first round of salt shed inspections summer 2026 and winter 2026, reporting back around May 2027
Joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send Ehlers presentation on residential TIF qualifications to commissioners
Electric vehicle charging stations to be future agenda item in fall 2026

title: Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an introductory meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received comprehensive overviews of department missions, budgets, and current initiatives including homelessness response, veteran services, and nutrition programs.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (remote participation noted)
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04 - missions, duties, responsibilities
Health Department educational presentation
Health Department budget overview
Social Services Department overview
Veteran Service Office presentation
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation
Next meeting date and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's four divisions: environmental health, nursing (communicable disease and family health), community health improvement, and administration. She highlighted 1,200 licenses overseen, public health vending machines stocked with free tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program's first graduates, and a new FTE position for homeless system response. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE staff and a 2026 budget of about $5 million, with roughly half from tax levy.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was absent. He described the department's divisions including child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. With nearly 140 FTE, it's the second largest department. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program and noted potential restructuring requests coming due to increased need in areas like child support.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported the office increased to 3.0 FTE and had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900. He noted veterans received $2.6 million in retroactive VA payments and $44 million annually in benefits. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts after 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county have its own VSO.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He described services including information assistance, caregiver support, dementia care, falls prevention, Medicare counseling, and Meals on Wheels. Ray noted significant financial challenges with federal nutrition funding flat since 2024 while food costs increased 40-45%, requiring meal and staffing reductions. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135.

Chair Boots noted the county completed its comprehensive plan last session and will develop a new strategic plan by December. He indicated he will bring strategic plan items to future meetings to ensure committee work aligns with county goals.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested future agenda items
Next meeting scheduled for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)

title: Marathon County Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris from the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The board heard emotional testimony from affected Ringle residents requesting fee waivers for disaster cleanup.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of meeting notice
Roll call of 38 supervisors
Committee appointments presentation and vote
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10-C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26: Social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fees modification for disaster relief
Announcements and adjournment

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders, and respecting supervisor preferences. He noted 34% of the board is in their first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli and seconded by Supervisor Crims was carried but not unanimous. Supervisor Enzy noted he was marked absent but wanted to be recorded as voting aye.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Supervisor Leur because she had seen her advocate for the county's smallest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors voted for two candidates. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for disaster debris following the tornado and flooding. The proposal reduces fees from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton by waiving the state fee ($13.10), town of Ringle fee ($2.33), and reducing to the commercial rate. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county wave all fees. Leonard mentioned exploring a partnership with the Community Foundation to help residents with unreimbursed costs. The board voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. Passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County wave all fees for disaster victims, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their fees, and that private businesses have donated $40,000+ in cleanup efforts. She requested a six-month timeline. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, noting some insurance adjusters haven't arrived yet and requesting more time and fee relief for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop a long-term policy for handling similar disaster situations within six months
Administrator Leonard to explore partnership with Community Foundation for reimbursing residents' out-of-pocket disposal costs
Staff to track all disaster-related debris with certification forms for potential state disaster aid documentation

title: Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the full county board that will only cover septic system reconnect fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comment
Approval of March 31, 2026 committee minutes
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for a land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature, and Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make the non-conforming home use conforming. He confirmed no natural areas would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather victims
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado affecting the towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about potential levy impacts. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to victims of non-declared disasters and proposed an amendment to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems rather than all fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution to forward to the county board also passed, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented an overview of solid waste operations including the landfill's history since 1980, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy launched in 2023, and challenges with leachate treatment due to PFAS regulations requiring on-site treatment by 2032. He noted significant capital expenses including $6 million for 11-acre construction and $3-4 million closures. Marilyn Bend praised the department's work helping Ringle tornado victims.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He covered the joint city-county department structure with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees, 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and Nine Mile County Forest recreation. A study showed out-of-town visitors generate $3.25 million annually for Marathon County. Supervisor Sefelt announced Lovelin's retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. No one signed up for public comment and no one spoke during the public hearing portions for either rezoning petition.
actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only reconnect fees for septic systems for tornado-damaged properties
Forward James Offer/David Tract rezoning approval to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning approval to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable
Conservation department to provide special educational meeting on conservation topics in summer

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to the nuisance ordinance, recommended an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, approved fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure. Public comment raised concerns about the new farmers market location and parking on grass potentially violating village code.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Minutes from previous meetings
Department reports - Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
Proposed amendments to Chapter 50 Nuisances
E-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82 Traffic and Vehicles
Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements
Address corrections for E911 compliance
Raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: Code Enforcement Officer Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement list after the property sale was completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported 2,394 police calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The police department has hired its 40th officer. Two SROs work with code enforcement during summer for zoning violations and pet licensing. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including tree limb height requirements of 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion focused on emotos (electric dirt bikes). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion included whether electric scooters used by children should be exempted, with the committee deciding to rely on officer discretion. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend approval with the addition of prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously. Rothschild has not yet approved; Schofield declined to participate.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities which typically use 5-hour windows. Michael from Public Works suggested potentially eliminating overnight parking and just signing 6-8 problem areas. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking, noting $10 citations help clear streets before plowing begins. Committee decided to table the item until July so Michael can identify specific exception areas.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest parking lot. Committee discussed whether to install a basic crosswalk ($2-3K) or lighted system ($25-30K). Committee consensus was that Mount Olive should pay for installation if they want it. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend board approval if Mount Olive agrees to cover costs passed unanimously.

Fire department presented regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. One inspection and sticker would be valid across all participating municipalities. Fire Marshal Marty already inspected one truck that failed due to improper LP piping. Wausau opted out to continue their own inspections. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue near a new duplex development. Chief Hunt noted an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval.

Structure has been abandoned since 2010-2011 and on code enforcement since 2021. Fire department previously declined to do a training burn due to unsafe conditions. Owners had housed chickens in the abandoned structure. Current owners prefer demolition with costs added to their tax roll rather than paying out of pocket. They are current on taxes. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to authorize raze order passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding the new farmers market location, expressing concern that parking vehicles on grass would destroy the park and potentially violates village code prohibiting grass parking. He noted ADA handicap spaces are signed on grass rather than adjacent asphalt, creating accessibility issues for wheelchair users. He questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks and raised concerns about mobile food vendor regulations on public property. He also criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, noting some have been pending for years with no visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 Nuisances amendments to Board of Trustees for May agenda
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition to Board of Trustees
Michael to develop list of specific areas requiring even/odd parking signage for July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing; if agreed, proceed to board approval
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for Delicoski, Ross, and Radkey properties to proceed
Staff authorized to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property condition at Sternberg and South Timber

title: Tourism Commission
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Village of Weston Board of Trustees meeting was scheduled to address a significant increase to park and recreation impact fees through a public hearing, along with numerous license renewals, multiple ordinance amendments, and several infrastructure construction projects. The meeting also included the Board of Review being adjourned to a future date and consideration of a new police department and municipal court facility site plan.

agenda.item: Public Hearing and Consideration of Ordinance No. 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015 to Amend Park and Recreation Impact Fees
Approval of April 13, 2026, Special Board of Trustees Meeting Minutes
Approval of April 21, 2026, Board of Trustees Meeting Minutes

Work Product Transmittals including April Building Permits, Budget Status, Code Enforcement Report, and Water Utility PSC Annual Report

Intergovernmental Agreement for Military Road Construction
Construction Services Proposals for Alderson and Jelinek Intersection Project
Construction Services Proposals for Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fees Public Hearing
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to hold a public hearing on proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development. The proposed changes would raise single-family fees from $300 to $761, duplex fees from $250 to $563 per unit, and multi-family fees from $250 to $495 per unit, based on the Village's 2022 Public Facilities Needs Assessment.

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: The Board was set to review and consider action on the site plan for a new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. This represents a significant public safety infrastructure project for the Village.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: The Board was expected to discuss feedback regarding the Farmers Market location at Kennedy Park and recommended follow-up actions. This item was designated for discussion only, with no formal action scheduled.

The Board was scheduled to consider a resolution expressing support for a long-term, sustainable funding solution to the State's Transportation Fund. This resolution would position the Village in ongoing state-level discussions about transportation infrastructure funding.
The Board was set to consider an intergovernmental agreement for Military Road construction, which would formalize cooperation between governmental entities on this roadway project.

discussions.item: Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider a raze order request for a property at 4104 Shorey Avenue, which would require demolition of a structure deemed unfit for habitation or use.

discussions.item: Mobile Food Vendors License Ordinance Amendment
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to consider Ordinance No. 26-008, which would amend Chapter 18 Business regulations regarding mobile food vendor licensing requirements.

The Board was set to consider renewal of numerous business licenses for the 2026-2027 licensing term, including adult establishment, weights and measures, cigarette/tobacco/vaping, lodging, various liquor licenses, and salvage licenses.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda, allowing any person to address the Board for up to three minutes regarding non-agenda items. Additionally, a formal public comment period was scheduled as part of the public hearing on park and recreation impact fees.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on Ordinance No. 26-010 amending park and recreation impact fees
Scheduled to vote on Resolution 2026-015 amending the Village fee schedule
Expected to consider approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Scheduled to vote on consent agenda including vouchers and multiple license renewals

Scheduled to vote on Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation fund solutions
Expected to consider site plan approval for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility
Scheduled to consider intergovernmental agreement for Military Road construction

Scheduled to consider raze order request for 4104 Shorey Avenue

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Board of Trustees was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026, to conduct a public hearing on proposed park and recreation impact fee increases, consider multiple ordinance amendments affecting mobile food vendors, committee structures, nuisances, and bicycle regulations, and address various infrastructure projects including the Mountain Bay Metro Police Department facility and road construction agreements.

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to hold a public hearing on proposed significant increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development. Single-family fees were set to increase from $300 to $761, duplex fees from $250 to $563 per unit, and multi-family fees from $250 to $495 per unit, based on the 2022 Public Facilities Needs Assessment.

discussions.item: Ordinance No. 26-008 Mobile Food Vendors License
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider amendments to Chapter 18 Business regulations regarding mobile food vendor licensing requirements. The specific changes to the licensing framework were set for trustee review and potential action.
The Board was scheduled to review proposed amendments to the administrative code regarding the structure and organization of standing committees within Village government.
The Board was set to consider amendments to Chapter 50 of the municipal code governing nuisance regulations within the Village.
The Board was expected to review proposed changes to traffic and vehicle regulations specifically related to bicycle use under Article III of Chapter 82.
The Board was scheduled to consider a resolution expressing support for long-term, sustainable funding solutions to address Wisconsin's transportation fund challenges at the state level.

As unfinished business, the Board was set to continue discussion on proposed amendments to Section 1.08 of the Elected and Appointed Village Officials Handbook regarding policies for remote participation in meetings.

discussions.item: Site Plan Review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department Facility
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to review and potentially act on the site plan for a new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, representing a significant public safety infrastructure project.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: The Board was set to discuss feedback regarding the Farmers Market location at Kennedy Park and consider recommended follow-up actions, though this item was designated for discussion only without formal action.

discussions.item: Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider a raze order request for a property at 4104 Shorey Avenue, which would require demolition of an existing structure.

discussions.item: Intergovernmental Agreement for Military Road Construction
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to consider an intergovernmental agreement related to Military Road construction, involving coordination with other governmental entities on road infrastructure.

discussions.item: Construction Services Proposals for Alderson and Jelinek Intersection Project
discussions.body: The Board was set to review and potentially select construction services proposals for improvements to the Alderson and Jelinek intersection.

discussions.item: Construction Services Proposals for Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider construction services proposals for the reconstruction of Bloedel Avenue, a significant road infrastructure project.

The Board was scheduled to consider renewal of multiple business licenses for the 2026-2027 term, including adult establishment, tobacco, lodging, alcohol, and salvage licenses through the consent agenda process.

publicComment: Public comment was scheduled for non-agenda items with a three-minute limit per speaker. Additional public comment opportunity was included as part of the public hearing on park and recreation impact fees.

title: Board of Trustees
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Board of Trustees was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026, to consider significant changes to park and recreation impact fees following a public hearing, review numerous license renewals for the 2026-2027 term, and address multiple infrastructure projects including construction proposals for intersection and road reconstruction projects.

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fees Public Hearing
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to hold a public hearing on proposed significant increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development. The proposed changes would raise single-family fees from $300 to $761, duplex fees from $250 to $563 per unit, and multi-family fees from $250 to $495 per unit, based on the Village's 2022 Public Facilities Needs Assessment.

The Board was expected to consider a resolution expressing support for a long-term, sustainable funding solution to Wisconsin's Transportation Fund. This resolution was set to address state-level transportation infrastructure funding concerns.

discussions.item: Site Plan Review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to review and potentially act on the site plan for a new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. This represents a significant public safety infrastructure project for the community.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: The Board was set to discuss feedback on the Farmers Market location at Kennedy Park and recommended follow-up actions. This item was designated for discussion only with no action expected.

discussions.item: Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider a raze order request for the property at 4104 Shorey Avenue. Raze orders typically require demolition of structures deemed unsafe or in violation of building codes.

The Board was scheduled to consider Ordinance No. 26-008 amending Chapter 18 Business regulations regarding Mobile Food Vendors License requirements. This was set to update licensing procedures for food truck and mobile vendor operations.

The Board was expected to review and act on construction services proposals for two major infrastructure projects: the Alderson and Jelinek Intersection Project and the Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project.

discussions.item: Intergovernmental Agreement for Military Road Construction
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to consider an intergovernmental agreement related to Military Road construction, which typically involves coordination with neighboring municipalities or county government on shared roadway projects.

The Board was set to discuss potential interest in completing CliftonStrengths assessments and training, a professional development tool designed to help identify individual strengths and improve team dynamics.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda, allowing persons to address the Board for up to three minutes regarding non-agenda items, with time extensions permitted at the Chief Presiding Officer's discretion. Additional public comment opportunity was scheduled during the Park and Recreation Impact Fees public hearing.

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

Approve the Minutes

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation 19.85 (g)
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Reading of the Mission Statement
Excellence in Action: Maine Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Approve Consent Agenda (Action Requested)
Old/Recurring Business
New Business
Open Forum
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

title: Wausau Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: Wausau City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Arbor Day Proclamation
Week of the Young Child Proclamation
National Preservation Month Proclamation
Workers Memorial Day Proclamation
March 24, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
April 14, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
